title: Recommendation Systems
description: Core recommendation system paradigms — collaborative filtering, matrix factorization, two-tower DNNs, multi-stage ranking, exploration strategies, cold start solutions, and offline/online evaluation.
details: RecSys spans collaborative filtering (neighborhood methods), matrix factorization (SVD, ALS), and deep learning (two-tower DNNs). The two-tower architecture separates user and item representation, enabling offline precomputation of item embeddings and fast ANN search. Multi-stage ranking (retrieval → ranking → re-ranking) balances recall and precision. Key challenges: cold start, exploration vs exploitation, feedback loops, and evaluation gaps between offline metrics and online A/B tests.

sections.title: Problem Setup & Data
sections.description:
A recommendation system predicts which items a user will engage with. The core data is a user-item interaction matrix:

Key challenges: implicit vs explicit feedback (binary clicks vs 1-5 ratings), cold start (new users/items have no history), exploration vs exploitation (show known-good vs try novel items), and feedback loops (the model biases what it sees, which biases future training data).

sections.title: Collaborative Filtering
sections.description:
[Intuition] Collaborative filtering assumes users who agreed in the past will agree in the future. User-based: find users similar to the target user, recommend what they liked. Item-based: find items similar to what the user liked, recommend those. Item-based is more stable (item similarities change slowly).

Key similarity metrics: cosine similarity (ignores rating scale — two users who rate 4-5 and 2-3 on the same items can be perfectly correlated if normalized), Pearson correlation (mean-centers first — accounts for each user's bias), Jaccard similarity (for binary data — overlap / union). Neighborhood selection: K nearest neighbors (typically 20-50).
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity

# User-item matrix: rows=users, cols=items, values=ratings
# Fill unknown with 0 for nearest neighbor computation

class ItemBasedCF:
    def __init__(self, n_neighbors=20):
        self.n_neighbors = n_neighbors
        self.sim_matrix = None

    def fit(self, U_I_matrix):
        # Item-item similarity via cosine (normalized rating vectors)
        self.sim_matrix = cosine_similarity(U_I_matrix.T)
        np.fill_diagonal(self.sim_matrix, 0)  # Don't recommend same item

    def predict(self, user_idx, item_idx):
        # Weighted sum of neighbors' ratings
        similarities = self.sim_matrix[item_idx]
        # Get top K neighbors who rated this item
        neighbor_items = np.argsort(-similarities)[:self.n_neighbors]
        weights = similarities[neighbor_items]
        ratings = U_I_matrix[user_idx, neighbor_items]
        if np.sum(weights) == 0:
            return 0
        return np.dot(weights, ratings) / np.sum(weights)

sections.title: Matrix Factorization
sections.description:
[Intuition] Instead of comparing raw user-item vectors, matrix factorization learns latent factors — hidden features like "action vs drama" or "comedy vs dark tone." Each user and item gets a K-dimensional embedding. The rating is predicted as the dot product: r̂ᵤᵢ = μ + bᵤ + bᵢ + qᵢᵀ · pᵤ where μ is global mean, bᵤ is user bias, bᵢ is item bias.

[Concrete Numbers] Netflix Prize: 100M ratings, 480K users, 18K items, 20 latent factors. The winning solution (SVD++, 2008) combined matrix factorization with neighborhood methods to achieve 10% improvement over Netflix's own system.

Key algorithms: SVD (stochastic gradient descent on observed ratings), ALS (Alternating Least Squares — fix user embeddings, solve for items, then vice versa, guaranteed convergence). ALS is better for implicit feedback and can be parallelized across workers.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np

# Manual SVD matrix factorization via SGD
# Netflix Prize: 100M ratings, 480K users, 18K items, 20 factors

class SVD:
    def __init__(self, n_factors=20, lr=0.01, reg=0.02, n_epochs=20):
        self.n_factors = n_factors
        self.lr = lr
        self.reg = reg
        self.n_epochs = n_epochs

    def fit(self, ratings, n_users, n_items):
        # Initialize
        self.bu = np.zeros(n_users)              # User bias
        self.bi = np.zeros(n_items)              # Item bias
        self.p = np.random.normal(0, 0.1, (n_users, self.n_factors))   # User factors
        self.q = np.random.normal(0, 0.1, (n_items, self.n_factors))   # Item factors
        self.mu = np.mean(ratings[:, 2])          # Global mean

        for epoch in range(self.n_epochs):
            np.random.shuffle(ratings)
            for u, i, r in ratings:
                u, i = int(u), int(i)
                pred = self.mu + self.bu[u] + self.bi[i] + self.q[i] @ self.p[u]
                err = r - pred
                # SGD update
                self.bu[u] += self.lr * (err - self.reg * self.bu[u])
                self.bi[i] += self.lr * (err - self.reg * self.bi[i])
                self.p[u] += self.lr * (err * self.q[i] - self.reg * self.p[u])
                self.q[i] += self.lr * (err * self.p[u] - self.reg * self.q[i])

    def predict(self, u, i):
        return self.mu + self.bu[u] + self.bi[i] + self.q[i] @ self.p[u]

# For implicit feedback, use ALS (Alternating Least Squares):
# Fix U, solve V: V = (U^T U + λI)^{-1} U^T R^T
# Fix V, solve U: U = (V^T V + λI)^{-1} V^T R

sections.title: Two-Tower DNN
sections.description:
[Intuition] The two-tower model enables scalable recommendation by splitting the model into two independent encoders. The query tower encodes user features (history, demographics, context), while the item tower encodes item features (metadata, content embeddings). Their similarity is the dot product: s(u,i) = f_θ(u) · g_ψ(i). Since item embeddings can be precomputed offline, serving becomes an ANN search problem — just compute the user query vector and find the nearest item embeddings.

Training uses sampled softmax loss: P(i|u) = exp(s(u,i)) / (exp(s(u,i)) + Σⱼ exp(s(u,j))) where j are negative samples. Negative sampling strategies: uniform (random items), in-batch (other users' positive items), or hard negatives (items similar but not clicked).
sections.codeBlock:
import tensorflow as tf

# Two-tower model with sampled softmax loss

class TwoTowerRecommender(tf.keras.Model):
    def __init__(self, num_users, num_items, embedding_dim=64):
        super().__init__()
        # Query tower: user features -> 64-dim
        self.user_embedding = tf.keras.layers.Embedding(num_users, embedding_dim)
        self.query_mlp = tf.keras.Sequential([
            tf.keras.layers.Dense(128, activation='relu'),
            tf.keras.layers.Dense(embedding_dim),
        ])
        # Item tower: item features -> 64-dim
        self.item_embedding = tf.keras.layers.Embedding(num_items, embedding_dim)
        self.item_mlp = tf.keras.Sequential([
            tf.keras.layers.Dense(128, activation='relu'),
            tf.keras.layers.Dense(embedding_dim),
        ])
        # For sampled softmax: item bias terms
        self.item_bias = tf.keras.layers.Embedding(num_items, 1)

    def call(self, user_ids, item_ids, training=False):
        user_emb = self.query_mlp(self.user_embedding(user_ids))
        item_emb = self.item_mlp(self.item_embedding(item_ids))
        scores = tf.reduce_sum(user_emb * item_emb, axis=-1)
        scores = scores + tf.squeeze(self.item_bias(item_ids), axis=-1)
        return scores

    def get_user_embedding(self, user_ids):
        return self.query_mlp(self.user_embedding(user_ids))

# Training with sampled softmax:
# For each positive pair (u, i+), sample K negative items (i1, i2, ..., iK)
# Loss = -log(exp(s(u,i+)) / (exp(s(u,i+)) + sum(exp(s(u,ij)))))

sections.title: Multi-Stage Ranking
sections.description:
[Intuition] Running the full deep model on 1M items is too expensive. Multi-stage ranking progressively filters items from broad to narrow using increasingly expensive and sophisticated models.

[Concrete Numbers] HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) achieves O(log n) ANN search with >99% recall@100. FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) provides GPU-accelerated batch search.
sections.codeBlock:
import faiss
import numpy as np

# FAISS HNSW index for fast ANN search
n_items = 1_000_000
embedding_dim = 64

# Build HNSW index on item embeddings
item_embeddings = np.random.random((n_items, embedding_dim)).astype('float32')

index = faiss.IndexHNSWFlat(embedding_dim, 32)  # 32 neighbors per node
index.hnsw.efConstruction = 200  # Build quality (higher = better recall)
index.add(item_embeddings)

# Serve: encode user query, search
# user_emb = query_tower(user_features)  # 64-dim
user_emb = np.random.random((1, embedding_dim)).astype('float32')
index.hnsw.efSearch = 64  # Search depth (higher = better recall, slower)

# Retrieve top-1000 candidates in <10ms
scores, indices = index.search(user_emb, 1000)

# These 1000 candidates then go to the ranking model

sections.title: Exploration vs Exploitation
sections.description:
[Intuition] Exploitation maximizes short-term reward (show what the user is likely to engage with). But without exploration, you'll never discover better items, suffer from filter bubbles, and can't learn about new items. The balance is critical.

Strategies: ε-greedy (with probability ε, show a random item — simple but naive). UCB (Upper Confidence Bound: score = μᵢ + √(2·ln(t) / nᵢ) — items with few impressions get a bonus, decays as nᵢ grows). Thompson sampling (maintain a posterior distribution over each item's CTR, sample from it — Bayes-optimal, state of the art).

[Concrete Numbers] Netflix's interleaving experiments: A/B testing requires large sample sizes (weeks for ±1% impact), while interleaving (show both models' rankings merged) detects differences 2-5× faster.

sections.title: Cold Start
sections.description:
[Intuition] The cold start problem arises because collaborative methods need interaction history. Two flavors:

User cold start (new user, no history): Use demographic features (age, region, device type), onboarding signals (selected interests, onboarding items rated). During training, dropout collaborative features — randomly mask user history features so the model learns a content-based fallback. This way, even users with zero history get reasonable recommendations from their demographics alone.

Item cold start (new item, no interactions): Use metadata embeddings (title, description, category tags, image features). Predict initial popularity based on similar items' launch-day performance. Gradually transition from content-based to collaborative as user interactions accumulate.
sections.codeBlock:
import tensorflow as tf

class ColdStartModel(tf.keras.Model):
    """Two-tower with dropout on collaborative features for cold start."""
    def __init__(self, num_items, embedding_dim=64):
        super().__init__()
        self.user_content = tf.keras.Sequential([
            tf.keras.layers.Dense(128, activation='relu'),   # Demographics
            tf.keras.layers.Dense(embedding_dim),
        ])
        self.user_collab = tf.keras.Sequential([
            tf.keras.layers.Dense(128, activation='relu'),   # History embeddings
            tf.keras.layers.Dense(embedding_dim),
        ])
        self.item_tower = tf.keras.Sequential([
            tf.keras.layers.Dense(128, activation='relu'),
            tf.keras.layers.Dense(embedding_dim),
        ])

    def call(self, user_features, item_features, training=False):
        content_emb = self.user_content(user_features['demo'])
        collab_emb = self.user_collab(user_features['history'])
        if training:
            # Randomly mask history with probability 0.3
            mask = tf.random.uniform(tf.shape(collab_emb)) > 0.3
            collab_emb = collab_emb * tf.cast(mask, tf.float32)
        # Blend: model learns to fallback to content when history is missing
        user_emb = content_emb + collab_emb
        item_emb = self.item_tower(item_features)
        return tf.reduce_sum(user_emb * item_emb, axis=-1)

sections.title: Evaluation
sections.description:
[Concrete Numbers] Netflix reports NDCG@10 of 0.85 for their production model — a 0.01 improvement translates to millions in user engagement.

Offline evaluation metrics: Recall@k (what fraction of held-out interactions were in the top-k), NDCG@k (position-weighted — a relevant item at rank 1 is worth more than at rank 10), MAP (mean average precision across users — combines precision and rank).

[Practical Workflow] Offline metrics are just the first gate. The real test is online via A/B testing. For more sensitivity, use interleaving — present both ranking models' results interleaved in a single list, then measure which model's items get more clicks. Interleaving detects 1% improvements in days vs weeks for A/B.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np

def ndcg_at_k(y_true, y_pred, k=10):
    """Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain@k"""
    # y_true: binary relevance for each item
    # y_pred: predicted scores
    top_k = np.argsort(-y_pred)[:k]
    dcg = 0.0
    for i, idx in enumerate(top_k):
        rel = y_true[idx]
        dcg += (2**rel - 1) / np.log2(i + 2)  # position 1 = log2(2)
    # Ideal DCG: sort by actual relevance
    ideal = np.sort(-y_true)[:k]
    idcg = sum((2**(-rel) - 1) / np.log2(i + 2) for i, rel in enumerate(ideal))
    return dcg / idcg if idcg > 0 else 0.0

def recall_at_k(y_true, y_pred, k=10):
    top_k = np.argsort(-y_pred)[:k]
    hits = np.sum(y_true[top_k])
    total = np.sum(y_true)
    return hits / total if total > 0 else 0.0

sections.title: FAANG Q&A — Recommendation Systems
sections.description:
[Interview Angle] RecSys questions test your understanding of the full pipeline: data, modeling, serving, evaluation.

  1. Netflix L4: "Compare collaborative filtering vs content-based recommendation." — CF: uses user-item interactions, finds patterns across users, suffers cold start, provides serendipity. Content-based: uses item features, no cold start for new items, but can't discover cross-category interests. Hybrid approaches combine both.
  2. Meta L5: "Explain the two-tower model — how do you train it and how do you serve it?" — Train: query tower processes user features, item tower processes item features, dot product gives score, trained with sampled softmax loss and negative sampling. Serve: precompute item embeddings offline with FAISS HNSW, compute user embedding online, ANN search to find top items.
  3. Google L5: "How does negative sampling work for training recommendation models?" — For each positive pair, sample K negative items. Uniform sampling is simple but weak (most items are obviously negative). In-batch negatives (other users' positives) are stronger. Hard negatives (items the model almost recommended) are most informative but risk overfitting.
  4. Amazon L4: "What's the cold start problem and how do you solve it?" — New users/items have no interaction history. Solutions: use demographic/content features, dropout collaborative features during training to force content-based fallback, multi-armed bandit for exploration, predict initial popularity via metadata.
  5. Netflix L5: "How would you evaluate a recommendation system offline?" — Use recall@k, NDCG@k, MAP. But offline metrics don't capture exploration, diversity, or user satisfaction. The true test is online A/B testing or interleaving. Interleaving is more sensitive — detects 1% improvements faster than A/B.

sections.title: References
sections.description:
• Covington, Adams, Sargin 2016 — Deep Neural Networks for YouTube Recommendations (YouTube DNN paper)
  • Koren, Bell, Volinsky 2009 — Matrix Factorization Techniques for Recommender Systems (Netflix Prize)
  • Malkov, Yashunin 2016 — Efficient and Robust Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search using Hierarchical Navigable Small World Graphs (HNSW)
  • Johnson, Douze, Jégou 2019 — Billion-scale Similarity Search with GPUs (FAISS)
  • Rendle et al. 2019 — Neural Collaborative Filtering vs. Matrix Factorization Revisited
  • Bennett, Lanning 2007 — The Netflix Prize (KDD Cup)
  • Yi et al. 2019 — Sampling-Bias-Corrected Neural Modeling for Large Corpus Item Recommendations
